Here's a thought:
Inefficiency in an electric device is heat, which, in the case of a heater, is beneficial. 

Excess sound is just movement of air which, in a fan, is also beneficial. 

So isn't a quiet toroidal fan quiet because it's moving less air?

author: @GreatWhiteNiko
text:
In Texas in summer (100F) designs like that lasts about 30 min.  Also in the open air you can barely feel anything. In an enclosed space (a van for example) it works fine but opening the doors kills the cooling effect instantly.
Using a military cooling vest that circulates cold water in a plastic tubing pressed against your skin also lasts a very short time (20 min tops with 2 lbs of ice).  Plus it has the problem of cooling the body locally - let's you work long hours as if nothing but actually the head ends up overheating.  A full cooling outfit that you cab buy  - pants, vest, hood - starts to be a ridiculous solution.

author: @mytubehkjt
text:
Swamp coolers add humidity and are not efficient. You need a heat exchanger to pump the cold water through then blow the air over/ through that.

Also, the majority of the cooling energy comes from the phase change of the ice to water. By not having the water go through a heat exchanger you're losing a lot of efficiency.

Also, they don't last long...

author: @kreynolds1123
text:
Here is a thought. Use a desicant that removes moisture from the air and is made of ions that can move charges around. Increase the surface area of the dessicant in a sponge like substrate and blow air through it. Dessicants like calcium chloride, sulpheric acid and others  cool down as it absorbs moisture from the air. 

Passing current between two plates generates hydrogen and oxygen. Effectivly regenerating the desicant. One can store the hydrogen for a fuel to use later. But the air now has less water and requires less energy to cool with an air cinditioner and less water condensing on the evaporator (cooling) coils.  

My AC condenses about 10 gallons of water on hot sunny days. If the humidity were reduced such that only 5 gallons condense. it would save my AC unit over 41 BTU of latent heat of condensation.

author: @leefoster4133
text: No, that's not efficient as its a modified swamp cooler.  I haven't given that a go myself but have seen videos on it.  It's called a vortex cooler.  What you do is you send air through a tube which passes through a heat exchanger (oil, water, etc connected to some radiator) and then on the output you have a larger diameter than the incoming side which creates a cooling effect.  No pumps, no expensive coolant, etc; just the fan that blows in or pulls out.  Just pipes through a heat exchanger on the outside.  IE, 3cm on the in side and 6-8cm on the output.  Another one is where the air goes into a 200 litre tank of water with 3cm pipes that coil from the bottom up.  The water is the heat exchanger media where you take the hot water from the bottom and route that to a water heater inlet.  Then you just take water from the utility which is cold to cool down the heat exchanger.  The last one which is the most efficient but no one really likes.  That to use a hobbit style home/earth ship home or alike where you just have a southern exposure where you can control the amount of light coming in and that's far more cheaper than anything else.  Problem is you have to mow the roof as it would have grass or what-not on it with 2 metres of soil. Also keeps the home warmer in the winter as well.  The last one is used for standard homes where you run the pipe out in the yard in a 15m x 15m area about 2 metres down and you just use the ground as your heat exchanger.

author: @docink6175
text: swamp coolers like this work great in dry ,arid climates but not in more humid/tropical areas. Ive tried several here in Florida, they do feel cool blowing on you but the air is quickly saturated . A heat exchange style works a little better but also uses a LOT of ice. I do like the soft side idea and with a  small portable power bank you can use it anywhere

replies.author: @russellzauner
replies.text:
Then modify it to be a water extractor, then it will also cool because that's how water extractors (dehumidifiers) work.
I know I can't make one at home (or I don't know how) but a TEG can be used to generate cold as well as heat and you don't need much - yes the other side generates heat but you're only using a little bit (not trying to be a CPU chiller, for instance) only enough to create a thermal differential that starts pulling all the water out of the air.
If you have access to compressed air (or can focus enough wind maybe) I've used a Ranque-Hilsch vortex tube to freeze water as well as almost burn myself on the exhaust.  Maybe we can create a few degree difference in the collected air which is enough to create a "rechiller" to hold the air until it's cold enough to make a noticeable difference at the "cold" exhaust port (of course I'd vent the "hot" exhaust outside, unless it was winter then I'd have a valve to swap ports).
If you have enough surface area and the proper materials you can also make fully passive cooling surface which will create a space that will always register cooler than the outside temp - it reflects one band of heat and leaks another (preferably in the human/machine range of emissions; also flip it over in the winter).

author: @lajosruhzek2459
text: Not really an air conditioner, although it might work for some cooling needs so long as you have ice on hand. Although I'm rather confused about why it would need a containment unit that is thermally insulated. Isn't the idea to use the coldness in the ice to cool down the area? It seems like the ice might stay unmelted longer, but it would also hinder it's cooling capacity. What would make more sense is if you insulated the space you were trying to cool rather than the ice itself.

replies.author: @Alacritous
replies.text: You want the cold to go where you want it, not to just bleed out into the area around the container.  Contact with the surface under the container will be wasteful for example.

replies.author: @lajosruhzek2459
replies.text: @Alacritous It won't make much of any difference because the fan will be circulating the cold air anyway, meaning that the air temperature is going to average out. Which is also why I said, *maybe* I could see insulating the area you wanted to cool down.

replies.author: @Alacritous
replies.text: ​@lajosruhzek2459​No. It won't. Cold bleeding into the surface that the container is sitting on won't do anything to the air. Air that is cooled coming into contact with the wall of an uninsulated container will just drop to the floor and disperse uncontrolled.

author: @RTR-MATEY
text: Air Conditioner ≠ Swamp Cooler (Evaporative Cooler)...Air Conditioner removes humidity from the air whilst cooling, thus "conditioning" the air, whereas a "Swamp Cooler" blows moist cool air in a hot dry environment absorbing the heat and pulling it away. A "Swamp Cooler" does not work in a humid environment!!! I live in Mississippi and we get a heck of a lot of moisture off the GoM blowing inland...Swamp Cooler doesn't work. BUT, I run a "Swamp Cooler" indoors in the wintertime. Figure out that riddle!!!

replies.author: @simongross3122
replies.text:
Actually, that's true.  Our airconditioner has a cycle that only dehumidifies.  Just running that in summer (in Sydney) can reduce the indoor temperature quite a lot.

Perhaps your swamp cooler in winter works well because where you live you don't really get a cool season - only a humid season (summer) and a dry one (winter).  Just guessing.

replies.author: @RTR-MATEY
replies.text: @simongross3122 The "Swamp Cooler" doubles as a humidifier in the winter when the air is dry...water is pumped over a resin paper grid as the warm air is pulled through it. Unlike a steam humidifier, the evaporative cooler/humidifier only humidifies the air to the extent that the heat/temperature will allow it to!!! So besides humidifying, it's also cleaning/washing the air being pulled through the pumped vertical water grid as well as circulating the water in the reservoir below so it doesn't stagnate and buildup a mold hazard. For every winter that I've run this setup, none of the five family members in our household have had colds or respiratory issues!!!

author: @l0I0I0I0

author: @replikvltyoutube3727
text:
This 'aircon' is really bad design because all that evaporated water humidifies already humid air, and makes it both feel hotter than it is but also hard to breathe.

The solution I like is a closed loop with a cheap aquarium pump, which pumps cold water (maybe with ice) from  bucket into a copper pipe spiral around fan, and back down to bucket

author: @justtinkering6713
text:
Swamp cooler? 
That adds humidity. Why not put copper or aluminum pipes inside the bucket,on the bottom. In one side out the other side. Put the ice and salt on top. Close the bucket. Blow air through the pipes. Cold but not humid.

replies.author: @ari197
replies.text: interestingly, this idea should also work as heater during winter time .. but instead of ice, you put in hot water ..

replies.author: @Hamish_A
replies.text: Or freezer blocks. They could also be put in a small frame so that air passes easily between them to improve efficiency.

author: @dougpine4746
text: Long time ago I knew a lady who used a large plastic bowl and ice with a fan in her kitchen. I was quickly educated why I was wrong about the melting ice adding moisture into the air even as the bowl was filling with melted water. Can you figure it out?

replies.author: @RTR-MATEY
replies.text: Moisture in the air condenses on the ice and adds to the melted water.

replies.author: @dougpine4746
replies.text: @RTR-MATEY wait watch and see how many people don't understand. Swamp coolers don't use ice but many people in humid climates have been dissatisfied with them. They are made for dry arid climates.
